common_name: Purple Glory Tree (Brazilian Spider Flower, Princess Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after last frost in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Purple Glory Tree grows rapidly and should be planted in a location with room to develop a multi-branched canopy 15–25 feet wide. In zone 9a, it freezes back hard in cold winters but resprouts vigorously from the trunk or base in spring; in zone 9b-10, it is semi-evergreen to evergreen. The most spectacular bloom occurs in fall (September–November) and a second flush in spring; do not prune in late summer to preserve flower buds.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, slightly acidic, organically rich soil; Purple Glory Tree grows most vigorously in full sun but tolerates afternoon shade in zone 9's intense summer heat. Tibouchina granulosa is a large, fast-growing tree from the Atlantic rainforest of Brazil, producing one of the most spectacular purple floral displays of any tree in the subtropical world. In late summer through fall, the entire canopy becomes covered with large (2–3 inch) royal purple to intense violet flowers borne in broad, flat-topped clusters at every stem tip — the effect is breathtaking, with a well-established specimen appearing as though it has been draped in a cascade of purple velvet. Each flower has prominent purple anthers on curved filaments that give the genus the common name 'Spider Flower.' The large, velvet-textured, dark-green, prominently ribbed leaves are attractive year-round. Tibouchina granulosa (the tree form) is distinguished from the shrubby Princess Flower (T. urvilleana, 6–10 feet) by its potential tree height of 25–40 feet in zone 10-11 and its tendency to develop a single dominant trunk. In zone 9, hard pruning after frost damage stimulates vigorous regrowth and heavy flowering on new wood. An outstanding specimen tree for subtropical Gulf Coast landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tibouchina species for Florida
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Tibouchina granulosa
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tibouchina granulosa cultivation
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Trees for Gulf Coast Louisiana: Tibouchina

common_name: Pink Powderpuff (Surinam Powderpuff, Pink Mimosa Bush)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after last frost in full sun to partial shade; Pink Powderpuff grows rapidly in warm, humid conditions and benefits from placement against a south or west wall in zone 9a for added frost protection. In zone 9a, freeze damage is common but the plant usually resprouts from the base in spring; in zone 9b, it blooms nearly year-round. Prune to shape immediately after the heaviest bloom flush; avoid heavy pruning in fall. Excellent as a large container specimen on a warm patio.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Pink Powderpuff thrives in the heat, humidity, and seasonal drought of zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, performing even better in zone 9b–11 where frost is rare. Calliandra surinamensis is a multi-stemmed tropical shrub or small tree native to Surinam and Brazil, producing the distinctive Calliandra flower type: spherical to semi-spherical clusters of numerous elongated stamens that collectively form a powder-puff appearance — in this species a beautiful bicolor combination of rose-pink at the outer stamen tips fading to white at the center. The individual flowers are small but borne simultaneously in numerous rounded clusters along the branches, and the overall effect of a plant in full bloom is a profusion of softly glowing pink puffballs distributed throughout the canopy. Flowers appear throughout the warm season in zone 9 and nearly year-round in zone 10-11. The feathery, twice-pinnate foliage is ornamentally attractive even out of bloom, giving the plant a delicate, lacy texture that contrasts beautifully with bold-leaved tropical neighbors. Highly attractive to hummingbirds, which probe the stamen clusters for nectar. Distinguishable from the Red Powderpuff (C. haematocephala) by its pink-and-white rather than solid red flowers and more compact habit.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calliandra surinamensis (Pink Powderpuff)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Calliandra surinamensis
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Calliandra species for Gulf Coast landscapes
LSU AgCenter — Hummingbird Attracting Tropicals for Louisiana

common_name: Beach Naupaka (Sea Lettuce Tree, Scaevola, Beach Fan Flower)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in full sun to partial shade in sandy, well-drained coastal soil; Beach Naupaka is exceptionally salt-spray tolerant and perfectly adapted to the hostile conditions of beach dunes, coastal embankments, and barrier island planting where salt wind defeats most ornamentals. In zone 9a, plants may be damaged by hard frost but typically resprout from the crown. Excellent for coastal stabilization and native coastal landscaping in zone 9b-11.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any sandy or well-drained soil — Beach Naupaka is one of the most salt-tolerant and wind-tolerant coastal shrubs available, performing on the front line of beach dunes and oceanfront plantings where few plants survive. Scaevola taccada is a large, spreading, salt-tolerant shrub native to tropical Pacific and Indian Ocean coastlines, found naturally on sandy beaches, dunes, and coastal margins from Hawaii to Australia, through Southeast Asia, and naturalized on Gulf Coast and Florida beaches. The distinctive white to pale lavender, fan-shaped flowers — each one appearing as if cut exactly in half, with all five petals gathered on one side (a characteristic of the Goodeniaceae family that ensures specific pollinator access) — are borne prolifically throughout the warm season. The thick, fleshy, succulent-textured, bright green leaves and extremely deep root system allow the plant to thrive with saltwater spray, drought, compacted sand, and periodic flooding. Beach Naupaka spreads by underground runners to form dense, stabilizing colonies that protect coastal dunes from erosion. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, it makes an outstanding low-maintenance native or naturalized coastal shrub for beach properties, waterfront landscapes, and salt-exposed sites where conventional ornamentals fail.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Scaevola taccada (Beach Naupaka)
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Scaevola taccada coastal restoration
Florida Native Plant Society — Scaevola taccada
USDA Plants Database — Scaevola taccada

common_name: Egyptian Papyrus (Nile Grass, Paper Reed, Papyrus Sedge)

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in shallow water (0–12 inches deep) or in constantly wet, boggy soil at the pond's edge in full sun to partial shade; Cyperus papyrus grows vigorously in zone 9 humid conditions and spreads by thick rhizomes. In zone 9a, the tops die back in hard frosts but rhizomes survive if the root zone stays above freezing — mulch heavily or maintain in water that doesn't freeze solid. Divide clumps every 2–3 years. Excellent in containers set in decorative water bowls or water features.

directions: Plant in shallow water, boggy pond margins, or constantly wet soil in full sun to partial shade; Egyptian Papyrus requires reliably moist to wet conditions and will decline rapidly if allowed to dry out. Cyperus papyrus is the famous tall sedge of the Nile Delta — the same plant used by ancient Egyptians to produce papyrus paper, one of the earliest writing materials, by splitting and layering the inner pith of the tall stems. In zone 9 Gulf Coast water gardens, it creates an extraordinary tropical effect: triangular, bright green stems rise 5–10 feet tall topped by enormous, spherical, light and airy umbels of thin radiating bracts tipped with tiny flower clusters — the silhouette of a giant, architectural feather duster or sparkler held at the top of a pole. Multiple stems from a single clump sway gracefully in breezes, creating constant motion. The plant also produces smaller stem-tip cuttings that can be inserted upside-down in water to produce offsets (whip propagation). In the humid Gulf Coast climate, Egyptian Papyrus grows with exceptional vigor in water gardens, pond margins, rain gardens, and boggy corners of the landscape. It is naturalized in parts of the Gulf Coast and Florida where escaped plants colonize canal banks and wetland margins.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cyperus papyrus (Egyptian Papyrus)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Aquatic Plants for Texas Water Features
LSU AgCenter — Aquatic and Bog Plants for Louisiana Gardens
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Cyperus papyrus

common_name: Natal Bulbine (Orange Bulbine, Red Bulbine, Cat's Tail)

planting.note: Plant transplants or direct sow seeds in spring in well-drained, sandy or gritty soil in full sun to partial shade; Natal Bulbine is drought-tolerant once established and requires excellent drainage — it rots in heavy, poorly-drained clay. It blooms most heavily in cooler weather (fall through spring in zone 9) but continues producing scattered flowers through summer. Can be divided in early fall. In zone 9, it remains semi-evergreen through mild winters; hard frosts kill the foliage but plants resprout from the fleshy roots.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, sandy, or amended soil; Natal Bulbine is a heat-tolerant, drought-resistant South African succulent perennial particularly well suited to zone 9 Gulf Coast conditions. Bulbine natalensis is a clump-forming succulent perennial from Natal province, South Africa, producing loose rosettes of narrow, fleshy, succulent, grass-like leaves and tall, slender spikes of small star-shaped flowers in vivid orange or yellow, each individual flower distinguished by fluffy yellow stamens that give it a bearded appearance. It blooms prolifically in fall through spring in zone 9 (September–May), then rests somewhat in summer heat while maintaining attractive foliage. The orange-flowered form is the most commonly grown and makes a striking groundcover or mass planting with its warm coral-orange flower spikes rising above the textural foliage. The yellow-flowered species B. frutescens (Stalked Bulbine) is also widely grown and produces more of a shrubby habit. The succulent leaves of Bulbine natalensis contain a clear gel similar to aloe vera that has traditional use in South African folk medicine for skin conditions, burns, and wounds. Excellent combined with ornamental grasses, agaves, and other drought-tolerant plants in zone 9 xeriscape plantings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bulbine frutescens and B. natalensis
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Bulbine for Texas Xeriscape Gardens
South African National Biodiversity Institute — Bulbine natalensis
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Bulbine natalensis
